title: Chapter 20: The Other Primordials
content: They came on the tenth day.
Not through the campus gates. Not through the Nether-Grid Exchange. They came through the sky.

The violet-black canopy split open like a wound, and three figures descended. They were not human-shaped. They were concepts given form: War, a walking furnace of molten bronze and screaming metal. Conquest, a figure of living gold with a hundred eyes that each saw a different future. Death, a woman in a white dress whose face was a beautiful, endless stillness.
The three Primordials who had sealed the Void.
The three who had trapped Silas.
The three who had ruled the Nether-Grid for twelve thousand years.
They landed in the main quad. The ground cracked beneath them. The buildings groaned. Everyone on campus felt them before they saw them — a weight in the chest, a pressure behind the eyes.
Roxanne walked out of the Fine Arts building to meet them.
Silas flanked her right. The Executioner flanked her left. The two Enforcement units she'd purchased stood behind her.
"Well," she said, looking at the three monstrous figures on her lawn. "This is dramatic."
War spoke. Its voice was the sound of a battlefield. "Heir of the Void. Surrender the card. You are not ready to wield it."
"I've been doing pretty well so far."
"You've been lucky. Luck is not a strategy."
"What's your strategy? Threaten me on my own campus, in front of my own people, and hope I give up?"
Conquest's hundred eyes blinked in sequence. "We are not here to threaten. We are here to warn. The Void has spoken to you. It will try to convince you to open it. Do not listen."
"Why? What happens if I open it?"

Death spoke. Her voice was soft. Sad. "Everything ends. Not just this world — every world. Every dimension. Every possibility. The Void is not a door to a new reality. It is the absence of all reality. Opening it would erase existence itself."
Silas stepped forward. "You're lying."
War turned its molten gaze on him. "Traitor. You were sealed for a reason."

"I was sealed because I refused to help you maintain a system built on slavery and debt. You told the Heir's ancestors that the Void would destroy everything. You told them the same lie you told me. The Void doesn't destroy — it unmakes what is broken and creates anew."
"Same thing," Conquest said.
"Not the same thing. Breaking a vase and throwing away the pieces is destruction. Melting down a broken vase to make something new is creation."
Roxanne looked between them.
"So the three of you sealed the Void, sealed Silas, and ruled the Nether-Grid for twelve thousand years. And now that someone with the key has shown up, you're scared."
"We are cautious," Death said.
"You're scared."
War's furnace form blazed hotter. "You are one human with a card. We are Primordials. Do not mistake our patience for weakness."

"I don't. I mistake your demands for desperation. If you could take the card, you would have taken it already. You can't. Because the card is bound to my bloodline. And my bloodline is the only thing that can open or close the Void."
She smiled.
"So you need me. And you're terrified that I might choose a side you don't like."
None of them denied it.
"Good," Roxanne said. "Then let's talk."
The three Primordials did not enter the campus.
They stood at the edge of the barrier Roxanne had purchased, testing it with their presence. The barrier held. It rippled where their aura touched it, but it held.
"Impressive barrier," War said. Its voice grated like grinding metal. "Purchased?"
"Eight million Obols. Worth every one."
"You spent eight million on a temporary energy shield."
"I spent eight million on peace of mind. There's a difference."
War's molten form flickered. "You speak as if currency has no meaning."
"To me, it doesn't. I have infinity. The only question is what I choose to spend it on."
"And you choose to spend it on walls."
"And medical clinics. And coffee machines. And freed gods."

Conquest stepped forward. Its hundred eyes blinked in a staggered wave. "You freed the Bound God. You purchased the Enforcement Division. You took the Second Seat. You are accumulating power at an alarming rate."
"I'm accumulating leverage. There's a difference."
"There isn't."
"There is. Power is the ability to destroy. Leverage is the ability to negotiate. I don't want to destroy the Nether-Grid. I want to change it."
The Exchange had a rhythm. Roxanne was learning to feel it.
Every transaction created a ripple. Every contract signed shifted the balance somewhere. The whole system was an enormous, interconnected web of debt and credit, and she was now a node at its center.
"We need to move faster," she said, walking through the campus. "The Council will regroup. They'll find a loophole. We need to secure more territory, more resources, more leverage before they do."
"You can't buy everything at once," Silas said.
"Watch me."
She held up the card.
*DISPLAY: ALL AVAILABLE DEBT CONTRACTS — EASTERN SECTOR.*
The screen filled with thousands of entries. Personal debts. Business debts. Soul contracts. Each with a buyout price, each representing a person or entity bound to the old system.
*TOTAL: 47,293 CONTRACTS. TOTAL BUYOUT: 12,400,000 OBOLS.*
"Purchase all."
*APPROVED. 12,400,000 OBOLS DEBITED. 47,293 CONTRACTS FORGIVEN.*
The Exchange feed updated. A wave of green icons spread across the map — debts cleared, obligations dissolved.
All across the city, people felt it. A weight lifting. A chain breaking. A sudden, inexplicable freedom.
Roxanne pocketed the card.
"That's a start."

Momentum was building. Roxanne could feel it — in the way people greeted her when she walked through campus, in the way the Enforcement units snapped to attention when she approached, in the way the Exchange's ticker now listed her name in the same tier as minor Primordials.
She was becoming a power.
"Keep your head," Silas warned. "The Exchange rewards rising stars. But it also consumes them."
"I know."
"Do you?"
"You've told me three times this week. It's starting to sink in."
He gave her a look that was equal parts concern and amusement.
"I'll stop warning you when you stop giving me reasons to warn you."
"So never."
"Probably."
